item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: An occurrence at REDACTED Creek Bridge

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like the only reason I downvoted was for the note at the end. Even so, I think it genuinely detracts from the rest of the article, taking a somber and quiet article and blasting it with cacophonous exposition.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So THIS is what happened to Ambrose Bierce?

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

So, in reading this article as part of the WikiWalk team duties, I was strongly reminded of SCP-909 in that someone, a writer no less, could literally cause a town to fall out of memory and be forgotten. While not exactly the same in terms of scope, the effects match up enough that an non-invasive inline link could bridge the two articles. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

The anomalous properties that seem to emanate from SCP-1084 are centered on the nearby village. Experimental subjects entering the village feel a strong sense of discomfort and guilt with no discernible cause, and invariably request to leave as soon as possible. All of the buildings showed significant weather damage consistent with a lack of upkeep over many decades. Perhaps most strangely of all, it is no longer possible for any human to recall or record the village's name; this has been confirmed by experimentation even at remote locations, as any conversation about the village including the town's name consistently results in all parties forgetting its name.

To this:

The anomalous properties that seem to emanate from SCP-1084 are centered on the nearby village. Experimental subjects entering the village feel a strong sense of discomfort and guilt with no discernible cause, and invariably request to leave as soon as possible. All of the buildings showed significant weather damage consistent with a lack of upkeep over many decades. Perhaps most strangely of all, it is no longer possible for any human to recall or record the village's name; this has been confirmed by experimentation even at remote locations, as any conversation about the village including the town's name consistently results in all parties forgetting its name.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The object itself is so weird, but the story's excellent.

item.title: Image Attribution
item.content:encoded: The image used in this article is a CC image from Flickr.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Some insight...
Hide

The whole SCP is a reference to Ambrose Bierce, about whose death you can read in more detail on your own time. The circumstances of his death were never specifically proven, and being a tough son-of-a-bitch as he was, lots of people want to attribute magical or supernatural circumstances to it simply because you can't prove it's not true. A lot of his writing dealt with horror and ghost themes, and I decided he had the power to curse a town with namelessness upon his death. That part is almost certainly not true.

The toasting-a-dead-author thing comes from this, which I just think is cool.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As I recall, this was actually supposed to be a specific historical figure (Ambrose Pierce, I think?) and all the effects are related to him and his death. And yes, part of the question here is why his curse did this, since other people's death-curses don't.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I…don't really get it. So an American acted like a standard American, a Mexican got offended by a simple cultural misunderstanding, the American was killed for it, and then the town was cursed because he said so as he died? Lots of people curse people/places/things as they die, don't they? What makes this person special? O_o And what the heck is up with the drinking and the specific date and stuff? None of this makes any sense at all…

Upvoted. ^.^

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't know if I'm missing something here but… why is this Euclid? It seems to me that the behaviour is predictable and not dangerous at all…
I'm upvoting though. Very well written.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like how I was directed to this through the "Recently Edited" tab, because it would have taken me forever to get to it how I was reading through all the SCP's. Well done Eskobar, still a great freaking article. HAVE MY +1 EVEN THOUGH IT IS LATE!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Whoop, good catch. Edit made.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"The rifles in the town still are still warm."

If there's no objection over the next few days, I think I'm going to take out one of those "stills"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Isn't pigdog… schweinhund… a German insult,rather than spanish?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, I'm properly creeped out. I like it. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was a mistake on my part. Mis-click!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Excellent stuff, Eskobar. Love it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
We do have a ton of other historical dudes.

Excellent work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay, I've Anglicized it. It was more atmospheric than anything, so easily disposed of.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: … Ambrose Bierce's corpse is an SCP now?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Bloody hell, Eskobar, this is actually quite creepy.
Good work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, that bugs me as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That said, the use of all of those terms in close proximity comes across as a little forced.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wondered this at first, too, but terms like gringo, blanco & yanqui have unique connotations in Spanish that don't really translate to the closest English terms. Abuela probably does mean "grandmother" here (and not "old woman") but it's an affectionate term, sort of like how some people call theirs "Gramma".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The author….Ambrose Bierce?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why would the diary translator leave some words untranslated for no apparent reason?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It turns out in the end that the whole SCP was really just the dream of a hanged man in his final moments before passing

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oooo…. A creepy paranormal SCP with a concrete effect, and a potential explanation that in-and-of itself is unexplainable…

I must admit, I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was concerned by your original downvote. Welcome to the home team!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I do like it. I don't know why but I really like it.
